dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for a possible cardiac event and reported Heart Problems/AICD at Patient First clinic.
Hi, I'm Mike, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having chest and throat pain with some shortness of breath. It started in the early afternoon around 2:30.
Can you tell me your name?
James Johnson.
And can you tell me today's date?
December 17th, 2019.
Where are you right now?
At Patient First Clinic in Richmond.
Your airway appears patent and there is no sign of obstruction.
16 breaths per minute, no signs of labored breathing. Your breathing is normal.
Radial pulse is 76 now, strong and regular. Blood pressure is 135 over 86. Your skin is warm and dry.
When did the pain start and can you describe it?
Around 2:30 PM today. It's a dull, constant pain. Right now, I'm rating it at 4 out of 10.
Does the pain get worse with certain movements or breathing?
Yes, it's worse with deep inspiration and when I lean over.
Are there any other symptoms you're experiencing?
I also have body aches that worsen with movement. I had some shortness of breath when the pain started, but it is milder now.
Any medical history or allergies?
I have a history of hypertension and take amlodipine and Triamterene/Hydrochlorothiazide for it. No known allergies to medications or foods.
What area is the pain in?
It's localized in my chest and throat.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate the pain now?
About a 4.
Your skin is warm, dry, with no rashes or discoloration noted. No pallor observed.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive at 3 mm. No drainage or swelling from EENT noted. You are alert and oriented 4/4.
Your breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally. Chest rises and falls normally.
Pulse is 76 per minute.
Respirations are 16 and unlabored. You had some shortness of breath earlier, but it's improved now.
Blood pressure is 135 over 86.
Oxygen saturation is 99%.
Blood sugar is 114 mg/dl.
We obtained a 12-lead EKG, and it shows sinus tachycardia without ectopy. Heart rate is stable at this time.

We have placed an IV in your left antecubital and given you 324 mg of Aspirin, as is protocol for suspected cardiac cause of chest pain. You are safe with this treatment unless you have any allergies, but you stated you have none to medicines or food.
We're now applying a 4-lead EKG for continued cardiac monitoring. We'll continue monitoring for stability during transport and review any changes if they occur.
We're implementing our chest pain protocol for suspected cardiac issues. We'll maintain ongoing EKG and vital sign evaluation until ED transfer.
Let me take your pulse again. It is now 121. Blood pressure rose to 138 over 100. This increase is a normal physiological response to pain and anxiety, but we'll continue monitoring closely.

Oxygen saturation decreased slightly to 98%, but remains within acceptable range. We do not see any signs of respiratory distress or hypoxia at this time. You are breathing easily and have no cyanosis to note now, as per our clinical examination.

Skin remains normal with no signs of swelling, redness, or discoloration to arms or legs. You aren't showing signs of internal bleeding or edema, and skin turgor is within expected normal range with no jaundice or bruising noted on re-assessment now or previously.

We're transporting you to St. Mary's Hospital in Richmond for further evaluation, as you reported ongoing chest and throat discomfort along with sinus tachycardia, which is common in cardiac conditions. They will run blood and do repeat EKGs if needed.
We'll assist you onto the stretcher now in a semi-fowler's position for comfort. We will place shoulder and lap straps for safe movement. Are you ready for transfer to the stretcher now?
Ready for transfer, yes.
Let me check your awareness again. What is your name?
James Johnson.

You are at the clinic and being transferred now. Do you see or feel anyone moving you or approaching you now with touch or obvious motion? That confirms you are physically placing yourself in this environment. Still understanding what I say now, not confused about this scenario. Thank you for being cooperative and helping us with this transfer safely. I see I got "Yes" and your name is correct, so AVPU all four points as alert and oriented 4/4. Glasgow score remains at 15 based on these responses now, no changes. GCS can show changes due to a cardiac syncope event, but you're stable for now.

Pulse is now 69, and blood pressure is 139 over 89. While these readings appear relatively stable, it's common to see fluctuations during baseline assessment and transport setup. We are keeping these in a monitored state throughout the transport journey for any recognizable changes.
Make sure your IV stays secure. Any issues on your side?
No, everything's fine.
Are you still having shortness of breath?
No, just the chest and throat pain.
EKG still shows sinus tachycardia with no changes noted. We will do repeat 10-lead ECGs if there is any abnormality during your transport.
We are starting transport now. Please remain still for safety during the trip to the hospital.
Safety straps are in place for securing your transport now.
You may experience motion, but we will monitor you all the way and ensure proper positioning, including the semi-fowler's method you are in now for comfort.

St. Mary's, we are transporting a 47-year-old male with chest pain, throat radiating pain, self-reported shortness of breath, and general body aches. ASA and IV administered at the scene. Vitals are stable, but sinus tachycardia is present. The patient remains alert and oriented throughout this process.
ED Room 12 is ready. Please provide pre-arrival report.

Pre-arrival report is en route. The patient remains en route with ASA on board and consistent tachycardia. Skin assessments over all surveyed areas had been noted as consistent and without issues. Both oxygen levels and other vitals "pass" basic standard. The patient has no threats to respiration or circulation as of now, but the cardiac event is ongoing.

Patient line is secure; we are en route for transport. IV line remains in good position and is functioning per flow as noted in past several checks. You will alert us if you note any issues with the IV or pain, so please let us know any increases or discomforts that may arise from the interventions or from positional changes during travel.

Patient remains fully alert and oriented with a Glasgow Coma Score of 15. We held a verbal evaluation, GCS 15 is per clinical standards at the moment and we are confident movement will not affect CNS function or GCS extraction. We can recheck throughout the trip for reassurances and able to resurvey upon arrival if needed.
No signs of jugular venous distention currently noted. Pupils still equal, round, reactive despite advancing time of event.
Ambulance departing the scene now. We will maintain contact with the destination so they are aware of our timing based on normal travel speeds with no current roadblock anomalies.

Patient is secured for transport including head, torso, and legs using recommended safety belt methods. New clinical data over a 12-minute time period demonstrated normal behaviors with no signs currently of internal or external fluid bleeding.

Flow is steady for the next two miles; no detours anticipated. Patient comfort and physiological status is intact by current data and observation, with no change in pulse, respirations, or status of EKG indicated.
Monitoring will continue during transport and will be relayed as per entrusting details to hospital regularly while in transit. Maintenance of EKG, IV, and oxygen monitoring as standard.
Vital signs remain stable, and pain level is 4. We will inform the receiving team if any new developments occur, as per the protocol guidelines.
All clinical parameters are consistent with the initial evaluation and treatment plan. Patient remains non-urgent considering reasons for transport.

No environmental changes reported and trip expected to take approximately four minutes. Travel speed is within expected baseline for region and time of night with no congestion evident in mapping current.

Maintain IV line for transfer; ASA was administered at the scene. No urgent reactions have been observed in the time since administration, nor any hypersensitivity states been indicated as this is per reported drug history.
Acknowledged. Prepare to receive in ED Room 12. All anticipated documentation and patient health status should be conveyed for integration in the ER.
Patient is en route as per EMS protocol with ongoing EKG monitoring in place and IV line active with no emergency status at this moment.

Full clinical summary dispatched for hospital check-in as reviewed with the current patient priority. All data fields were filed and uploaded at the start of transport for integration into implied receiving care.
Patient disposition: Active cardiac evaluation in progress per protocol. We remain available to the hospital or for any needs provided through the receiving unit within reach lines.
Patient remaining in semi-Fowler's position en route, with EKG monitoring, IV intact, and core vitals under observation. Any changes will be reported urgently.
Last check was ten minutes ago and the essentials recalculated and aligned with initial findings per baseline expectations with no significant variations as of yet.
Patient remains secured and stable during transport with no significant deviation detected at 3, 6, and 9-minute intervals during trip so far.

No repositioning or adjustment needed at this time based on visual and empirical review. All physical and reactive states unchanged from baseline assessment to upkeep reasonable confidence in transport safety. The patient has maintained his local posture per initial equipment plan.
